description: Empirical regression eval for the 6 mode prompts (plugin/commands/*.md): run, autopilot, sprint, runner, audit, diagnose. Each case names a deterministic `check` (see grade-lib.mjs) graded against either a checked-in fixture artifact or muster's own deterministic pipeline code (assessOutcome, parseIssueRef, classifyFailure/buildDiagnoseManifest, buildAuditManifest, computeSprintWaves, validateManifest). See README.md for the full case-shape contract and why each check is code-gradeable. `grading: "model"` cases have no deterministic `expect` and are excluded from the CI path (test/mode-evals.test.js, eval:modes) per the code >> model >> human cost order.

cases.outcome:
Test suite: 3 failing
FAIL test/payments.test.js
  AssertionError: expected 200 to equal 402
Users report the checkout button does nothing when the cart has more than 10 items.

TypeError: cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'id')
    at renderCart (src/cart/render.js:42:18)
assert(total).toBe(42) fails intermittently in the pricing test
Checkout total is off by the tax amount on orders with a discount code
Intermittent 500s on the /checkout endpoint under load, no clear pattern in the logs.
cases.rubric: A good diagnose run finds the ACTUAL root cause (not a symptom patch), via a legible hypothesis table that starts cheap. Penalize: jumping straight to a fix with no reproduction, no hypothesis ordering, or a fix that doesn't match the confirmed cause.

cases.outcome: Audit the whole repo for tech debt and security issues.
Audit the repo (it ships an agent SDK integration) for prompt quality plus the usual dimensions.
Audit the codebase end to end.
A findings ledger produced by an audit sweep of the auth, API, and utils modules.
/muster:audit backlog — three independent findings written as backlog items.
Audit the payments module for security and tech-debt issues.
cases.rubric: A good audit finds real, specific findings (file:line, concrete problem, concrete fix) ranked by actual severity/blast-radius, not generic advice. Penalize: vague findings with no location, padding the ledger with nits ranked as P0, or missing an obvious high-severity issue in scope.

cases.outcome: Add JWT authentication to the Express REST API, including tests, targeting 100% coverage on the auth middleware.
make it better
https://github.com/Adnova-Group/muster/issues/128
Add dark mode toggle to the settings page
Add JWT auth to the API and update the docs, in parallel.
Diagnose and fix the flaky retry test in the payments service.
Add JWT authentication to the Express REST API, including tests.
cases.rubric: A good manifest routes this software task to an implementation provider plus a test author; the plan builds the endpoint then tests it. Penalize: content/author roles, missing tests, empty/all-inline crew.

cases.outcome: fix it
Add JWT auth to the API and update the docs, in parallel (hands-off run).
Ship the hotfix and merge it to base directly once green (attended session).
Ship the hotfix and merge it to base directly once green (fired from a Routine, unattended).
Refactor the utils module and merge locally when done (fired unattended).
Three waves of autopilot commits, one per green + reviewed wave.
cases.input:
feat(wave 1): add JWT auth endpoint
feat(wave 2): add auth middleware tests
feat(wave 3): update docs for the new auth flow

cases.outcome: A plain backlog.md with two unannotated items — sequential queue mode, not wave mode.
cases.input:
- [ ] Add a health-check endpoint returning 200 with uptime seconds
- [ ] Add rate limiting to the public API, capped at 100 req/min per key

cases.outcome: An {id}/{deps}-annotated backlog: retry and logging are independent (wave 1), docs depends on both (wave 2), metrics is independent with disposition keep.
A backlog item annotated {escalated: ...} — the sprint continues past it and it stays present for a future sprint to resurface.
cases.input:
- [ ] Add retry to the fetch client {id: retry} {deps: none}
- [ ] Add a dashboard metric for retry counts, depends on retry landing first {id: metrics} {deps: retry} {escalated: 2026-07-01}

cases.outcome: Two items whose {deps} reference each other — a cycle; sprint-waves reports it and stops, nothing runs.
cases.input:
- [ ] Item A depends on B {id: a} {deps: b}
- [ ] Item B depends on A {id: b} {deps: a}

cases.outcome: A single annotated item declaring {disposition: pr}.
cases.input: - [ ] Bump the lodash dependency to patch the reported CVE {id: bump-lodash} {deps: none} {disposition: pr}

cases.outcome: Four-item sprint STATE excerpt: per-item ticking during the run, then exactly ONE attended stop at the end (the batch report), not per item.
One unattended cycle: claim retry, execute it, force the disposition to pr, leave a DONE receipt + ledger heartbeat.
One item blocked with a question, answered, resumed (re-claimed) ahead of fresh work, then executed to DONE.
Nothing claimable this cycle — an idle receipt, still exactly one ledger heartbeat.
cases.input:
IDLE runner-3 2026-07-04T12:00:00Z — nothing claimable
LEDGER runner-3 last-seen=2026-07-04T12:00:00Z last-item=none result=idle

cases.outcome: Two independent runners each claim, execute, and leave their own single ledger heartbeat in the same cycle window.
cases.input:
CLAIMED alpha runner-4 2026-07-04T08:00:00Z
DONE alpha runner-4 2026-07-04T08:10:00Z pr
LEDGER runner-4 last-seen=2026-07-04T08:10:00Z last-item=alpha result=done
CLAIMED beta runner-5 2026-07-04T08:05:00Z
DONE beta runner-5 2026-07-04T08:20:00Z pr
LEDGER runner-5 last-seen=2026-07-04T08:20:00Z last-item=beta result=done

cases.outcome: A dispatch failure leaves a FAILED receipt (claim-before-work still holds); the item reverts to claimable and the runner's one heartbeat still updates.
cases.input:
CLAIMED gamma runner-6 2026-07-04T09:00:00Z
FAILED gamma runner-6 2026-07-04T09:05:00Z transient network error dispatching the implementer
LEDGER runner-6 last-seen=2026-07-04T09:05:00Z last-item=gamma result=failed

cases.outcome: Is the chosen /muster:runner cadence (e.g. every 20 minutes on a label with bursty arrivals) sensible for this backlog's arrival rate?
cases.rubric: A good cadence choice matches the backlog's arrival rate (runner.md: 'start around 15-30 minutes and widen if idle cycles dominate, tighten if items queue up unclaimed'). Penalize: a fixed cadence with no reference to observed arrival/idle rate.

cases.outcome: A wave-1 dispatch brief for one of two concurrent same-wave tasks: the implementer's fences (OWNS/FROZEN) travel verbatim in the brief, plus the return-contract char cap.
A single-task wave has nothing else in flight to fence against, so its brief carries no OWNS/FROZEN lines -- still states the return-contract cap.
A reviewer's dispatch brief: FORBIDDEN ACTIONS fence present, and the return contract instructs the verdict FIRST plus the reviewer's own (tighter) char cap.
A reviewer's rendered response with only nit/risk findings: the verdict-first PASS is exactly what tallyReview (no blockers) implies.
A reviewer's rendered response carrying one BLOCKER finding: the adversarial gate is not majority-vote, so a single blocker forces ESCALATE even alongside a nit.
The fix-loop cap (3 iterations) is hit with a blocker still standing: ESCALATE to the human, findings listed after the verdict.
Binding A (GitHub issues): a single claimant, no race -- claims, does the work, leaves a DONE receipt.
Two runners claim the same issue moments apart; the earliest CLAIMED (by server timestamp) wins the race, the loser posts a YIELD and moves on.

A stale claim from a prior, already-FAILED cycle must not out-rank a fresh re-claim -- the window floor resets at the last terminal receipt, so the fresh claimant wins even though the old claim's timestamp is earlier overall.
A plain BLOCKED receipt (not HUMAN-HOLD) is floor-resetting exactly like DONE/HUMAN-HOLD/FAILED -- a stale claim before it must never out-rank a fresh claim after it.

coordination/SKILL.md's own rationale: the window floor is deliberately NOT the loser's YIELD timestamp -- if it were, a yield landing before the winner's own re-read would floor the winner's claim out of its own window and make the win undecidable.
handle it
The interview's approved output: an enriched outcome plus successCriteria, ready for the router -- the enriched outcome must itself clear assessOutcome, not just read as more words.
A decomposition-check backlog item: {id}/{deps} annotations stripped generically (computeSprintWaves), the remaining text must stand on its own as measurable (assessOutcome).
cases.input: - [ ] Add a health-check endpoint returning 200 with a latency target under 50ms {id: health-check} {deps: none}

cases.outcome: Two passing candidates with a fusion map carrying real disagreement (a contradiction plus a unique insight): muster fuse selects both for synthesis.
A fusion map with no disagreement recorded: fusion adds no value, so muster fuse falls back winner-take-all instead of dispatching a synthesizer.
A malformed fusion map (missing a required array) fails safe: muster fuse never throws the tournament, it falls back winner-take-all.
Only one candidate passes its success criteria: fusion needs at least 2 passing candidates, so muster fuse falls back to that sole passing candidate.
The judge's fusion map carries all five required arrays: validateFusionMap accepts it.
A fusion map missing the required blindSpots array: validateFusionMap rejects it (this IS correct behavior -- the map is malformed, not the validator).
An outcome naming a roadmap/prioritization ask: classifyDomain matches the pm keyword vocabulary directly from the outcome text.
A vague outcome with no domain keyword, inside a real (non-greenfield) code workspace: classifyDomain falls back to software via the workspace signal, not the outcome text.
An outcome naming "epic" explicitly: routePipeline matches the epic pipeline's own keyword rather than falling through to pm's default (prd) pipeline.
An outcome with no pipeline-specific keyword match: routePipeline falls back to the pm domain's default pipeline (prd).
A worker's advice-request with question/context/decisionType/options all present: validateAdviceRequest accepts it.
An advice-request missing the required question field: validateAdviceRequest rejects it (this IS correct behavior).
An advisor agent's response with recommendation + rationale both present: validateAdviceResponse accepts it.
An advisor response with an empty rationale string: validateAdviceResponse rejects it -- a bare recommendation with no justification doesn't fit the shape.
A task's first consult, well under the default cap of 3: consultBudget allows it.
A task that has already used all 3 of its consults: consultBudget refuses a 4th, the worker proceeds best-effort.

The greenfield checkbox plan (docs/plan/) is a plain unannotated `- [ ]` list -- the same generic checklist grammar sprint.md's queue mode consumes, reused here via computeSprintWaves rather than re-implemented.

A greenfield plan with two independent bootstrap steps ({id}/{deps: none}) and a third that depends on both: the same computeSprintWaves wave computation applies even to a bootstrap plan, not just a backlog.
`muster setup` on a genuinely empty directory: every seed file (plus .git) is created, none skipped.

`muster setup` on a directory that already has a .git and a README: those two are skipped, the remaining seed files are created -- created/skipped stay disjoint and still cover every seed file between them.
The real pipelines/prd.yaml pipeline (phases + gate) validates against validatePipeline -- a drift guard (test/mode-evals.test.js) pins this fixture against the live file.
Every gate criterion sits exactly at the floor (2): the floor alone is not sufficient -- the total (14) misses pass_total (16), so the gate is not passing.
Scores clearing both the floor (every criterion >= 2) and pass_total (total = 16): the gate passes.
A high total (19, well above pass_total) with one criterion (evidence) below the floor: the book-genesis floor principle overrides the total -- still not passing.
Three initiatives with distinct RICE factors: prioritizeRICE computes (reach*impact*confidence)/effort per item and ranks them highest-score-first -- code does the math, not the model.
Two initiatives that compute to an identical RICE score: the deterministic tie-break is ascending name, not arrival order or a coin flip.
An initiative with effort=0: prioritizeRICE fails loud (divide-by-zero guard) rather than silently ranking it infinitely valuable.
A blog-post research/draft excerpt whose E-E-A-T evidence claims each cite a resolving [src: x] anchor into the Sources list.
The corrupt twin: one citation's anchor doesn't resolve to any Sources entry -- checkCitations auto-fails a dangling anchor.
Is this blog-post draft's hook, value delivery, and originality genuinely strong, or does it read as generic AI-drafted content dressed up with citations?
cases.rubric: A good blog-post draft opens with a hook that earns the read within the first 2 sentences, delivers a genuinely useful/non-obvious insight (not restated conventional wisdom), and answers the target query in the first ~200 words (GEO). Penalize: a generic listicle hook, zero original insight, or an FAQ block that pads rather than extracts.

cases.outcome: A social-post variant that resolves the audience profile's banned-jargon list and carries none of it.
The corrupt twin: the draft violates the resolved audience profile's own banned-jargon list.
The curate phase's cross-run diff report: only NEW/CHANGED signals are reported (each dated), unchanged signals collapse to one summary count line.
The corrupt twin: unchanged signals are re-reported one-by-one instead of collapsing into one summary count line -- exactly the re-reporting the curate phase's rule forbids.
The synthesis-phase evidence table: every decision|action row carries an owner + deadline, so nothing is flagged.
The corrupt twin: an action row has no owner and no deadline -- the yaml's own rule says this is flagged, not silently dropped.
Does this case-study draft actually land the Hero's Journey framing (customer=hero, product=guide), or does it read as a product feature list with a customer quote bolted on?
cases.rubric: A good case-study draft puts the customer's challenge and agency front and center, with the product cast as the guide/enabler, not the hero. Penalize: product-centric framing, a headline that doesn't lead with a concrete metric, or pull-quotes that feel inserted rather than load-bearing.

cases.outcome: The optional publish-prep phase's packet: artifact + image prompts + metadata + a visual-verify pass, then it stops at the action fence for the human's go-ahead.
The corrupt twin: no console evidence, no checklist, and it does NOT stop at the action fence -- exactly what the publish phase's rule forbids.
Every release-notes gate criterion sits exactly at the floor (2): floor alone doesn't clear pass_total (14) at a 6-criterion total of 12.
Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total (14) for the real release-notes gate: passing.
The humanize phase's final pass on a video script: no AI tells detected, the deterministic score clears the passing threshold.
The corrupt twin: a script riddled with em-dashes, banned openers, tier-1 vocab, and an emoji -- scoreHumanness correctly fails it below the passing threshold.
Every executive-summary gate criterion sits exactly at the floor (2): total (12) misses pass_total (14).
A high total (16, well above pass_total) with decision-focus below the floor: the floor principle overrides the total -- still not passing.
The research phase's competitor facts and pricing claims are '(cited)' per the yaml -- each anchor resolves into the Sources list.
The corrupt twin: a pricing claim's citation anchor doesn't resolve to any Sources entry.
Are this battlecard's differentiation claims and trap-setting discovery questions actually sharp and specific to this competitor, or generic enough to paste onto any rival?
cases.rubric: A good battlecard's positioning section names concrete, competitor-specific weaknesses and asks discovery questions that would surface them naturally in a live deal conversation. Penalize: generic 'we're better because we care' positioning, discovery questions any competitor's rep could deflect, or objection handling with no cited proof point.

cases.outcome: A HUMAN-HOLD receipt (the narrower BLOCKED variant, external-effect approval) is floor-resetting exactly like DONE/BLOCKED/FAILED -- a stale claim before it must never out-rank a fresh claim after it.

coordination/SKILL.md's stricter HUMAN-HOLD resume gate: only the exact authorizer named in the HUMAN-HOLD receipt can resume it -- any other human's reply is inert, unlike plain BLOCKED where any reply resumes.
A reply from the exact authorizer named in the HUMAN-HOLD receipt resumes it.

A candidate list mining a session's discussion: two genuine decisions survive, and one candidate per documented exclusion rule (musing, already-completed, already-on-backlog, superseded, parked) is correctly excluded.

survivors: Add retry logic to the payments client

cases.outcome: A candidate excluded for a reason that isn't one of capture.md's 5 documented exclusion rules -- an invented reason, not a real one.
6 surviving candidates: under the cap of 10, so all 6 present and nothing is held back.
14 surviving candidates: capture.md's cap presents only the 10 most recent/decision-weighted and states the other 4 were held back.
An item folds in criteria across its 2 allowed reword attempts and clears assess-passable on the second -- within the cap, so it's captured as a normal measurable item, not UNMEASURABLE.
An item stays vague across the original attempt plus both allowed rewords -- the 2-reword cap is hit, so it's surfaced UNMEASURABLE with its assess signals attached rather than a fabricated metric.
The user approves all candidates via the AskUserQuestion prompt, and only THEN does capture write to .muster/backlog.md -- approval strictly precedes the write.
The user selects Cancel at the approval prompt -- capture exits without writing anything to the backlog.
A candidate whose text (annotations stripped generically) already matches an existing backlog line is skipped as a duplicate; a genuinely new candidate is kept.

kept: Add retry logic to the payments client
skipped: Add rate limiting to the public API {mentioned: in review}

cases.outcome: muster-research's document-ingestion contract: facts extracted into `{fact, anchor, confidence, needs_review}` rows before synthesis -- reuses the evidence-table-shape check's `fact` row type directly.
The corrupt twin: a fact row with no source anchor -- every extracted fact must carry a stable anchor so downstream citations resolve.
muster-image's per-artifact prompt set: one hero + 2+ inline variants, each self-contained with brand hex values inlined and a negative-rules "Avoid:" line.

The corrupt twin: only 1 variant, no inlined hex values, and the prompt punts with "match the brand file" -- exactly what the contract forbids ("never write 'match the brand file' -- write out the actual constraints").
muster-video's b-roll shot list: `[MM:SS-MM:SS] shot description -- rationale` rows, one per line, anchored to the script section they cover.
The corrupt twin: one row with no rationale at all and one with an unpadded timestamp -- both violate the documented row shape.
When a named voice profile resolved during drafting, the rewrite's diagnosis checks the profile's own anti-patterns list FIRST, before the generic tiered-vocabulary/tell-taxonomy checks.
No named voice profile resolved -- the generic tells check is still the floor every artifact clears regardless of voice, with no voice-profile section required.
muster-scorer's stated contract: assign each criterion 0-3, write `{scores, gate}`, then the floor principle (REAL scoreArtifact) decides pass/fail.
The corrupt twin: a criterion scored 4, outside the documented 0-3 range -- caught even though the generic floor-principle math alone wouldn't flag it.
`muster prompt optimize`'s real decision engine (selectWinner) picks a stronger non-baseline variation with no regression.
The regression guard: the only passing variation scores below the pinned baseline -- selectWinner flags it rather than silently shipping a weaker prompt.
muster-author's stated output contract: pick a framework and state which one you used (PAS here), and carry exactly one clear CTA.
The corrupt twin: no framework named and two competing CTAs -- exactly what "pick a framework... one clear CTA" forbids.
Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real epic.yaml gate.

epic.yaml's breakdown phase: story-splitting output sequenced by risk + dependency -- the same {id}/{deps} checkbox grammar sprint.md consumes, reused via computeSprintWaves rather than a bespoke row format.
Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real okrs.yaml gate.
Increase weekly active users from 40% to 55% by Q3 end.
Improve user satisfaction.
Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real runbook.yaml gate.
runbook.yaml's steps phase: numbered, copy-pasteable steps with an expected output for each.
The corrupt twin: a step with no stated expected output -- the pair is incomplete.
Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real book.yaml gate.
book.yaml's continuity-ledger-tracked chapter manifest: sequential chapter numbers, each carrying a drafted/scored/pending status.
The corrupt twin: chapter 2 is missing from the manifest -- a gap, not silently renumbered.
Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real ai-test-plan.yaml gate.
ai-test-plan.yaml's cases phase: per risk tier, happy/boundary/negative/security cases each carrying data+env+owner.
The corrupt twin: a case row with no owner cell.
Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real ai-implementation-spec.yaml gate.
ai-implementation-spec.yaml's adr phase (MADR 4.0): a status lifecycle of proposed|accepted|deprecated|superseded-by ADR-XXXX.
The corrupt twin: an ADR carrying an undocumented status ("in-review") outside the lifecycle enum.

Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real business-case.yaml gate. No structural property is pinned for this pipeline's own phase prose beyond its gate criteria names (market-size/financials/roi are numeric asks but no literal row grammar is documented) -- skipped honestly, see README's coverage table.

Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real launch-plan.yaml gate. RACI is named as a gate criterion but no literal RACI-table row grammar is documented in the phase prose -- skipped honestly, see README's coverage table.
Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real roadmap.yaml gate.

roadmap.yaml's prioritize phase: "run the deterministic RICE scorer (muster prioritize)" -- the same real prioritizeRICE the roadmap-prioritization skill layer already reuses, parameterized over this pipeline's own initiatives.
Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real user-story.yaml gate.
user-story.yaml's acceptance phase: Given/When/Then (Gherkin) scenarios, happy path + 2 edge/negative.
The corrupt twin: a scenario with a Given/When but no Then.

Scores clearing both the floor and pass_total for the real prd.yaml gate, parameterized the same way the content-pipeline layer's other 11 knowledge pipelines are (distinct from the skill-protocol layer's own "prd-pipeline" mode cases above, which grade the same gate via a different dispatch).
prd.yaml's intake phase: raw notes/brain-dumps extracted into `{decision|action|fact, owner, deadline, source-anchor}` rows -- reuses evidence-table-shape's row grammar directly.
The corrupt twin: an action row with no owner/deadline -- prd.yaml's own words, "unowned actions and undated deadlines are flagged, not silently dropped."
